mapQuestions.question: Which continent is Spain on?
mapQuestions.answer: Europe, in the south-west, on the Iberian Peninsula 🌍

mapQuestions.question: What is the capital of Spain?
mapQuestions.answer: Madrid. It sits almost exactly in the geographic middle of the country, on a high plateau.

mapQuestions.question: What sea and ocean touch Spain?
mapQuestions.answer: The Mediterranean Sea on the east and south, and the Atlantic Ocean on the north-west.

mapQuestions.question: Which small country is completely inside Spain (almost)?
mapQuestions.answer: Andorra and Portugal share its borders, but the tiny British territory of Gibraltar sits on the southern tip, an actual rock with monkeys on it.

mapQuestions.question: What is the highest mountain in mainland Spain?
mapQuestions.answer: Mulhacén, 3,479 metres, in the Sierra Nevada of Andalusia. Tide Peak on the Canary Islands is even higher, at 3,715 metres on a volcano.

foods.name: Paella
foods.description: Saffron rice cooked in a huge flat pan with seafood, chicken, rabbit or vegetables. Real paella is from Valencia and is only eaten at lunch, never dinner.

foods.name: Tapas
foods.description: Small dishes meant to be shared. Not a meal, a way of eating. Order three or four, share them all, then move to another bar and do it again.

foods.name: Jamón
foods.description: Cured ham, sliced paper-thin. The best is jamón ibérico from black pigs that eat acorns, hung in cellars for years. Tastes nutty, salty and unbelievably savoury.

foods.name: Churros con chocolate
foods.description: Fried dough sticks dipped in a cup of hot chocolate so thick a spoon stands up in it. Breakfast for grown-ups, after a big night out. Or for any kid, any time.

foods.name: Gazpacho
foods.description: A cold tomato, pepper and cucumber soup, drunk from a glass on hot days in the south. Sounds wrong on paper, tastes like summer.

foods.name: Tortilla española
foods.description: A thick potato omelette, served in slices. Eaten warm or cold, for breakfast, lunch or dinner. Spaniards argue over whether onion belongs in it, it does.

scavengerItems.label: A black silhouette of a bull on a hillside (the famous "Toro de Osborne" road sign)
A whole town that goes quiet around 2pm for siesta
An old man drinking wine at 11am at a tapas bar
A long string of dried red peppers hanging from a balcony
An orange tree growing on a street
A flamenco guitar or someone playing one in a square
The "paseo", the evening stroll where the whole town walks in a circle around the main square
A plaza mayor (main square) with arcades all the way around
An old white village clinging to a hillside (a "pueblo blanco")
A castle or fortress on a hilltop (Spain has more castles than any country in Europe)
A horseshoe arch left over from the Moors (most easily seen in Andalusia)
A flamenco poster outside a bar

geoMatches.description: The capital, high on a plateau. Royal palace, world-class art (the Prado), late dinners, and a buzzy nightlife that does not start until midnight.
Coastal Catalan city. Gaudí's mad buildings, the Sagrada Família still being built after 140 years, tapas, beaches and Gothic alleyways.
The hot, sun-baked south. Flamenco, white hilltop villages, orange trees in every square, and the Alhambra palace in Granada.
The green, rainy north. Its own language and identity, world-famous restaurants, sea cliffs, and pintxos (Basque tapas) on every corner in San Sebastián.
A volcanic archipelago off the coast of Africa. Black sand beaches, year-round sunshine, and the tallest mountain in Spain on Tenerife.
The wild green Atlantic north-west. Misty cliffs, bagpipes (yes, bagpipes), boiled octopus on wooden plates, and the end of the Camino pilgrim route.

senses.smell: Olive oil and garlic in a frying pan, jasmine in Andalusian patios, salty Mediterranean air, the leather of new boots in Toledo, fresh oranges on the trees…
senses.hear: Late café chatter that goes on until 1am, flamenco clapping ("palmas"), church bells, the rolled "rr" in every conversation, scooters in narrow lanes…
senses.taste: Salty fatty jamón, vinegary olives, sweet horchata, cold beer ("una caña") on a hot afternoon, chocolate so thick you eat it with a spoon, the spice of paprika…
senses.feel: White-hot midday heat that makes you go inside, the relief of a cool tile floor, the breeze on a balcony at midnight, smooth pebbles on a Mediterranean beach…
senses.see: What is the most incredible thing right in front of you right now…

stories.question: Why has Gaudí's church been under construction for 140 years?
stories.body: Antoni Gaudí started designing the Sagrada Família in 1882. He was so obsessed with it that he eventually moved into the workshop on site and refused to work on anything else. In 1926 he was hit by a tram on his way back from mass. He looked like a beggar in his old shabby clothes, so no one helped him at first, and he died a few days later. The church was still nowhere near finished, and Gaudí had only completed drawings for parts of it. They are STILL building it from his sketches and models, expecting to finish around 2026, 144 years after they started.
stories.thinkingQuestion: Gaudí knew he would never live to see his church finished. Have you ever started something that you knew someone else would have to finish for you?

stories.question: Why do Spaniards eat dinner at 10pm?
stories.body: In Spain it is totally normal to sit down for dinner at 10pm, even with kids. Most restaurants do not even open for dinner until 8.30pm. One reason: in 1942 the dictator Franco decided to put Spain on Berlin time to align with Nazi Germany during the war, but Spain is geographically much further west, so the clocks are basically wrong. Lunchtime in Spain is solar noon in most of Europe. The whole rhythm of the day, the siesta, the late dinner, makes more sense if you imagine the clocks an hour earlier.

stories.question: Why is there a palace with Arabic writing all over it?
stories.body: For nearly 800 years, much of Spain was part of the Muslim world, ruled by people who came over from north Africa in the year 711. They called the region "Al-Andalus", which is where the modern name Andalusia comes from. They built the Alhambra in Granada in the 1300s, with carved plasterwork, fountains, courtyards and Arabic poetry written on the walls. In 1492, the Christian kings finally pushed the last Muslim rulers out, but the Alhambra was so beautiful that they kept it almost intact. You can still walk through it today and read the poems carved into the walls.

stories.question: Why do thousands of people throw tomatoes at each other every August?
stories.body: Every August, the small town of Buñol in Valencia holds "La Tomatina", the world's biggest food fight. For one hour, around 20,000 people pelt each other with 150,000 over-ripe tomatoes in the streets, until the whole town runs red. It started around 1945 from a small fight at a parade, with no one really sure why it happened, but it kept going year after year because everyone had fun. Now you have to buy a ticket. The town fire engines hose everyone down afterwards.
stories.thinkingQuestion: If your town invented a yearly festival, what should everyone do for one hour, just for fun?

stories.question: What is the most famous painting in Spain?
stories.body: In 1937, during the Spanish Civil War, the small town of Guernica in the Basque Country was bombed for hours by German and Italian planes helping the Spanish dictator Franco. Most of the town was destroyed and hundreds of civilians died. A Spanish artist living in Paris, Pablo Picasso, was so horrified that he painted a huge mural in black, white and grey, showing screaming people and animals, broken bodies, a crying woman holding a dead child. It is called "Guernica", it is over 3 metres tall and 7 metres wide, and it now lives in the Reina Sofía museum in Madrid. Picasso said it should stay outside Spain until democracy returned. It only came home in 1981.
stories.thinkingQuestion: Picasso believed a painting could change how people feel about war. Do you think a picture can really change minds?

convoQuestions.question: What's the best thing you've eaten in Spain so far?
What's been the latest you've eaten dinner here? Did you love it or hate it?
Spain has tons of regional identities. Have you noticed somewhere that felt very different from somewhere else?
What's one Spanish habit you'd like to take home?
Have you tried to roll your "r"? Whose is best in our family?
Spanish kids stay up late and are part of grown-up nights out. What do you think the upsides and downsides of that are?

wordSearch.hint: Spanish foods, places and things to spot.

animals.name: Feral pigeon
animals.description: Every plaza, every cathedral square, every tapas terrace.

animals.name: White stork
animals.description: On almost every church tower in central / western Spain. Huge nests of sticks.

animals.name: Toro / fighting bull
animals.description: Bred on dehesa pastures across Andalusia and Extremadura. Watch for the Osborne bull billboards too.

animals.name: Greater flamingo
animals.description: Pink wading birds at coastal salt lagoons (Doñana, Fuente de Piedra).

animals.name: Spanish ibex
animals.description: Wild goats with curved horns on rocky slopes in the Sierra Nevada and Pyrenees.

animals.name: Iberian lynx
animals.description: Critically endangered wild cat with ear tufts. Real sightings are rare — count zoo or sanctuary too.
